coredata.dc:title: Target triggered fluorescence “turn-off” of silicon nanoparticles for cobalt detection and cell imaging with high sensitivity and selectivity
coredata.dc:creator: Nsanzamahoro, Stanislas
Cheng, Wei
Mutuyimana, Félicité Pacifique
Li, Ling
Wang, Weifeng
Ren, Cuiling
Yi, Tao
Chen, Hongli
Chen, Xingguo
coredata.dc:description: Cobalt ions (Co2+) are among heavy metals ions which cause pollution in environment because of their toxicity and improper degradation. In this work, a new fluorescent approach based on silicon nanoparticles (Si NPs) was designed for Co2+ detection. The fluorescent Si NPs were prepared by mixing 3-aminopropyl trimethoxysilane (APTES) and basic fuchsin, and under the excitation of 400 nm, they emitted green fluorescence at 515 nm. The prepared Si NPs were highly soluble in water, stable to salt and pH, and their fluorescence emission was extremely constant, with the quantum yield of 2.28%. The detailed mechanism studies showed that Co2+ effectively quenched the fluorescence of Si NPs by forming static complex. After optimizing the reaction parameters, a good linear relationship for Co2+ was observed from 0.2 to 60 μM, and the limit of detection was 0.14 μM that is lower than the guideline announced by Department of Environmental Protection for drinking water (1.7 μM). The preparation method of Si NPs was cheap, rapid and simple, and the fluorescent approach was applied to determine Co2+ in Yellow river water, drinking water, and industrial wastewater. Moreover, the Si NPs has good response to exogenous Co2+ in HepG2 cell imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silicon nanoparticles
Fluorescence “turn-off”
Co2+ measurement
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence “turn off” experiment for Co2+ measurement
Water sample preparation for Co2+ measurement
Sensitivity of Si NPs towards Co2+ measurement
Selectivity of Si NPs towards Co2+ measurement
Analysis of cobalt in water samples
Cytotoxicity assay and cell imaging of exogenous cobalt

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The synthetic strategy of Si NPs; (B) Schematic illustration of Co2+ detection by the synthesized Si NPs.
(A) TEM image of the synthesized Si NPs. (B) Size distribution of Si NPs. (C) FT-IR spectrum of the synthesized Si NPs.

High resolution XPS spectra of Si NPs. (A) Full range, (B) C 1s, (C) N 1s, (D) O 1s and (E) Si 2p, respectively. (F) Fluorescence excitation spectrum (a), emission spectrum (b), and UV–vis absorption spectrum of Si NPs (c). Inset photographs are Si NPs solutions under visible light and UV light illumination.

(A) FL intensity of Si NPs as function of time under 400 nm light illumination. (B) Normalized FL intensity of the Si NPs in 10 mM PBS (pH 7.4) with different concentrations of NaCl. (C) Normalized FL intensity of Si NPs after incubation at different temperature for 4 min. (D) Normalized FL intensity of Si NPs at various pH values. Error bars stand for the standard deviation of three independent experiments.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of Si NPs upon addition of various concentrations of Co2+ (from top to bottom, 0, 0.2, 1, 3, 5, 15, 25, 35, 45, 60, 90, 120, 160, and 200 μM respectively) in 10 mM PBS solution (pH 7.4). (B) Linear calibration plot between fluorescence quenching efficiency (F 0–F)/F 0 and the concentration of Co2+ (0.2–60 μM). Error bars stand for the standard deviation of three independent experiments.

(A) FL intensity (F 0–F)/F 0 response of the Si NPs to 400 μM metal ions (black bars) and the subsequent addition of 40 μM Co2+ (red bars). (B) (F 0–F)/F 0 response of the Si NPs to 400 μM anions (black bars) and the subsequent addition of 40 μM Co2+ (red bars). (C) (F 0–F)/F 0 response of the Si NPs to 400 μM molecules (black bars) and the subsequent addition of 40 μM Co2+ (red bars). Error bars stand for the standard deviation of three independent experiments. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Fluorescence microscopy of HepG2 cells incubated with (1) blank, (2) 20 μg⋅mL−1 Si NPs for 2 h, and (3) pretreated with Co2+ (100 μM) for 30 min, and then incubated with Si NPs (20 μg mL−1) for 2 h.

(A) FL excitation spectrum (red) and emission spectrum (black) of Si NPs, and UV–vis absorption spectra of Si NPs (blue), Co2+ (pink) and their mixture (green). (B) Lifetimes of steady-state FL of Si NPs and Si NPs + Co2+. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Analytical results for Co2+ detection in Yellow River water, industrial waste water and drinking water samples.
The quenching constants (K q) between Co2+ and Si NPs calculated by Stern-Volumer equation.

head.ce:title: Target triggered fluorescence “turn-off” of silicon nanoparticles for cobalt detection and cell imaging with high sensitivity and selectivity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou, 730000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources, Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS), Lanzhou, 730000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources
Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CAS Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources, Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS), Lanzhou 730000, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chinese Medicine, Hong Kong Baptist University, Hong Kong Special Administrative Region, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chinese Medicine, Hong Kong Baptist University, , P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cobalt ions (Co2+) are among heavy metals ions which cause pollution in environment because of their toxicity and improper degradation. In this work, a new fluorescent approach based on silicon nanoparticles (Si NPs) was designed for Co2+ detection. The fluorescent Si NPs were prepared by mixing 3-aminopropyl trimethoxysilane (APTES) and basic fuchsin, and under the excitation of 400 nm, they emitted green fluorescence at 515 nm. The prepared Si NPs were highly soluble in water, stable to salt and pH, and their fluorescence emission was extremely constant, with the quantum yield of 2.28%. The detailed mechanism studies showed that Co2+ effectively quenched the fluorescence of Si NPs by forming static complex. After optimizing the reaction parameters, a good linear relationship for Co2+ was observed from 0.2 to 60 μM, and the limit of detection was 0.14 μM that is lower than the guideline announced by Department of Environmental Protection for drinking water (1.7 μM). The preparation method of Si NPs was cheap, rapid and simple, and the fluorescent approach was applied to determine Co2+ in Yellow river water, drinking water, and industrial wastewater. Moreover, the Si NPs has good response to exogenous Co2+ in HepG2 cell imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, heavy metal ions detection in ecological samples has attracted more and more attentions, because of their toxicity and non-biodegradability. Some of heavy metal ions are indispensable to human body, where a few milligrams are only required to be consumed per day. Thus, it tends to be dangerous to human health once consumed in high level [1]. Cobalt (Co2+) is one of such kind of metal ions. In industries, Co2+ is used in batteries and pigments production [2]. Human activities and natural resources are the main ways for Co2+ to enter the environment [3]. Co2+ is mainly found in soils, minerals and rocks [4]; and it plays an essential part in ecological system [5,6]. The concentration range of Co2+ in environment is up to 100 μg L−1 in wastewater and 0.5–12 μg L−1 in sea water [7]. For both eukaryotes and prokaryotes, cobalt is an essential element [8]. It is indispensable in the synthesis of proteins, myelin, co-enzyme, amino acids and it helps in fats and carbohydrates metabolism [9–11]. In human body, the total presence of Co2+ is 1.1–1.5 mg where, 43% is found in soft tissues, 14% in bone and 43% is accumulated in muscles [12,13].

Nowadays, various approaches have been developed for Co2+ determination such as colorimetric approaches [9,14,15], fluorescent approaches [10,11,16], and potentiometric approach [13]. Some of these methods present disadvantages of high cost, less selectivity, low sensitivity and complex operational strategies [17,18]. Cobalt analysis via traditional methods also need trained individuals and experienced sample treatment steps [8]. Fluorescent-based methods have experienced extreme attention compared to other analytical sensors due to their sensitivity, selectivity, and simplicity [11].

Evolution of nanoparticles as chemical sensors raised attractive research interest in the past decades [19]. Heavy metals in nanoparticles are toxic, and this limited their applications in biochemical subjects [20]. Silicon is one of the elements that have been mostly used to synthesize nanoparticles especially silicon-based nanoparticles (Si NPs). Raw materials for Si NPs synthesis are comparatively abundant and low-priced because silicon is the second most available element on the earth. Radioactive combination of holes and electrons give rise to the fluorescence feature of Si NPs [20]. Electrochemical and mechanic features of Si NPs are based on their large surface-to-volume ratios and this allows them to be used in diseases treatment, nano agents for biological detection and analysis, and nanosensors [21]. As a zero-sized nanomaterial, fluorescent Si NPs gained increasing interest due to their features, especially low toxicity, favorable biocompatibility and photostability [19].

Fluorescent Si NPs have been synthesized by different methods [22,23]. Some of them are prepared through microwave method where high temperature processing is required [24,25]. Some are synthesized via two or more steps which require time consuming [26,27], while others are synthesized and then doped or functionalized with some row materials (peptides or metals) before their uses [25,28,29]. Therefore, synthesis of Si NPs with facile, cheap and easy procedures is still in high demand.

Here, we described an approach for preparing water-soluble Si NPs via one simple and easy step by using basic fuchsin as reductant and 3-aminopropyl trimethoxysilane (APTES) as silicon source (Fig. 1 A). The synthesized Si NPs demonstrated pH stability, salt stability, photo stability, favorable compatibility and strong green fluorescence. It was cleared that the green fluorescence emitted by Si NPs was directly quenched after the reaction with Co2+ (Fig. 1B). The proposed approach was successfully used to analyze Co2+ in water samples and sense exogenous Co2+ in HepG 2 cell imaging.

Except for other notation, all reagents were of analytical grade and were directly used without any further purification. 3-Aminopropyl trimethoxysilane (APTES), basic fuchsin, alanine, methionine, glucose, glutathione, hydroquinone, and resorcinol were received from Shanghai Titan Scientific Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). NaCl, NaBr, NaI, Na2CO3, Na2SO4, CaCl2, catechol and vitamin B12 were purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Chemical Reagents Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). NaNO3, KCl, LiCl, FeCl2, BaCl2, Mg(NO3)2, Ca(NO3)2, Ni(NO3)2, CuCl2, Zn(NO3)2, AlCl3, AgNO3, MnCl2⋅4H2O and Hg(NO3)2 were received from Sinopharm Chemical Reagents Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). In all the experiments, ultrapure water from 18202 V AXL water purification system (Chongqing, China) was used.

Fluorescence (FL) emission spectra were measured using RF-5301PC spectrofluorometer, assembled with 1 cm quartz cell (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectrum was performed by TU-1901 double beam UV–vis spectrometer, with 1 cm quartz cell (Purkine General Instrument Co. Ltd., Beijing, China). Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FT-IR) was measured on a Nicolet Nexus 670 spectrometer. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was recorded using D/max 82400 X-ray powder diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation. Size and morphology of the prepared Si NPs were demonstrated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi-600, and Hitachi, Japan). The sample solution of Si NPs (10 μL) were dropped onto a copper TEM grid and then dried overnight at room temperature. Time-resolved fluorescence spectra were measured on time-correlated-single-photon-counting (TCSPC) system from FL 920 P spectrometer (Edinburgh machine, UK) within an excitation wavelength at 365 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed by using a PHI5702 spectrometer assembled with Al Kα exciting source. pH values of the used buffer solutions were recorded using Mettler Toledo pH meter (Switzerland).

In our experiment, precursors for the Si NPs synthesis were APTES and basic fuchsin. Procedure for the synthesis was reported in the following ways: firstly, in the water bath of 70 °C, 1 mL of APTES was added to 5 mL of ultrapure water with stirring. Later on, 10 mg of basic fuchsin was dissolved into the above solution with continuous stirring. The total reaction time for the Si NPs synthesis was 70 min. Subsequently, the obtained Si NPs solution passed through a dialysis membrane of 1000 Da (molecular weight cutoff) against ultrapure water to remove small molecules and unreacted reagent for 8 h. Finally, the purified Si NPs solution was kept at room temperature for the further uses. Some purified Si NPs solution was freeze-dried to produce the dry powder of Si NPs, which was then grinded and crushed into a fine powder of Si NPs for XRD, FT-IR and XPS characterizations.

In order to illustrate the luminescent materials, only APTES or basic fuchsin as precursor for nanomaterials synthesis was also investigated, and the other processes were the same as the above mentioned.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence “turn off” experiment for Co2+ measurement
ce:section.ce:para: The measurement of Co2+ was conducted in PBS buffer solution. Typically, 40 μL of Si NPs solution was added into PBS buffer solution (10 mM, pH 7.4). This prepared solution was mixed with different concentrations of Co2+ ranging from 0.2 to 200 μM. The obtained mixture was incubated for 4 min at room temperature and the fluorescence emission signals were evaluated by setting the excitation wavelength at 400 nm. To perform the selectivity experiment toward Co2+, various metal ions (i.e., Li+, K+, Na+, Ag+, Ba2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Zn2+ and Al3+), anions (i.e., I−, Br−, Cl−, NO3 −, CO3 2− and SO4 2−) and biomolecules (alanine, glucose, glutathione, methionine, catechol, hydroquinone, resorcinol and vitamin B12) were tested under the same operation conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water sample preparation for Co2+ measurement
ce:section.ce:para: Industrial waste water sample was acquired from the effluent of an electroplating factory (Gansu, China). Drinking water sample was received from one of the supermarket in Lanzhou (Gansu, China). Yellow River water sample was collected from Lanzhou (Gansu, China). Industrial waste water and Yellow River water were centrifuged at 1200 rpm for 10 min and then filtered at 0.22 μM pore membrane. Before detection, all samples were diluted by 10.0 mM PBS buffer solution (pH 7.4). After incubation of 4 min at room temperature, the FL emission spectrum was investigated under the excitation wavelength at 400 nm.

The cytotoxicity of Si NPs was determined by the MTT (3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl-2-H-tetrazolium bromide) assay. Cells (5 × 103) were incubated with varying concentrations of Si NPs (0, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200 and 400 μg mL−1) in triplicate in a 96-well plate at 37 °C in a final volume of 100 μL. At the end of the treatment (20 h), 10 μL of MTT (5 mg mL−1) was added to each well and incubated for an additional 4 h at 37 °C. An extraction buffer (100 μL, 10% SDS, 5% isobutanol, 0.1% HCl) was added, and the cells were incubated overnight at 37 °C. The absorbance was measured at 570 nm on Multiskan GO (Thermo Scientific). The cell viability was expressed as the percentage of the control (cells without drug treatment).

In a humidified atmosphere of 5% CO2 overnight, the HepG2 cells were seeded in a 12-well plate and cultured in DMEM medium (1 mL/well) at 37 °C. To the cell culture, Si NPs (20 μg⋅mL-1) were added and then, cells were incubated for an additional 2 h at 37 °C. For the assessment of Co2+ uptake, HepG2 cells were pretreated with Co2+ (100 μM) for 30 min and then incubated with Si NPs (20 μg⋅mL-1) for 2 h. After removing the unbound compounds to the cells by rinsing them three times by PBS solution (10 mM, pH = 7.4), cells were ready for imaging experiments.

By virtue of the pH-sensitive property of basic fuchsin, Wang's group developed label-free carbon dots as ratiometric fluorescence pH nanoprobes through one-pot hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and basic fuchsin and applied them for intracellular pH sensing [30]. In this work, APTES and basic fuchsin were respectively used as silicon source and reducing agent. Fig. 1 demonstrated the synthetic approach of Si NPs. By mixing APTES and basic fuchsin in one step, Si NPs were prepared. At first, in order to illustrate the luminescent materials, the FL intensity of different molar ratio of APTES and basic fuchsin (0:1, and 1:0) as precursor for Si NPs synthesis was investigated. As shown in Fig. S1, under the excitation wavelength at 400 nm, the FL intensity of only basic fuchsin (line a) was negligible; that of only APTES was very weak with the emission wavelength at 515 nm (line b); and that of the mixed solution (a and b in the ratio of 1:1) was also very weak (line d). However, the emission peak with high FL intensity was observed from the synthesized Si NPs by APTES and basic fuchsin as precursors (line c). In a word, Si NPs were responsible for the further fluorescent analysis, and basic fuchsin was an excellent reductant similar to that used in the preparation process of carbon dots [30].

Highest FL intensities were received after optimizing three reaction conditions which are: the ratio of APTES to basic fuchsin, the reaction temperature and time. The impact of the molar ratio was investigated by fixing the volume of APTES to 1.0 mL and changing the amount of basic fuchsin. The FL intensity increased by raising the amount of basic fuchsin until to 10 mg, and then slightly decreased as the amount of basic fuchsin further increased (Fig. S2A). Figs. S2B and S2C demonstrated the effects of reaction temperature and time on the FL intensity. Firstly, there was an increasing of the FL intensity up to 70 min and 70 °C, and then the decreasing was observed. To sum up, 10 mg, 70 °C and 70 min were respectively chosen as APTES dosage, reaction temperature and time for further studies.

The size and morphology of Si NPs were described by the transmission electron microscopy (TEM). According to Fig. 2 A and B, the prepared Si NPs displayed good size uniformity and monodispersity with spherical shape. Furthermore, the variation of the size was between 1.1 and 2.7 nm, and the medium size was 1.8 nm. The synthesized Si NPs were in amorphous state, which was confirmed by XRD analysis (Fig. S3). The prepared Si NPs contained C, N, O and Si elements as indicated by EDS pattern in Fig. S4. Chemical groups available on the surface of the synthesized Si NPs were identified by FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2C). The absorption peaks at 3410 and 1602 cm−1 were assigned to the bending vibration of N–H [31]; those at 1125-1132 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of Si–O–Si [32], and those at 1461 and 2937 cm−1 belonged to C–H bending and C–H unsaturated stretching vibration, respectively [32]. The stretching vibration and deformation of O–H [33] belonged to the absorption peaks in the range of 2870–2932 cm−1. The above results demonstrated that the synthesized Si NPs were rich in amino and hydroxyl groups.

XPS experiment was conducted to further evaluate the elemental composition of the prepared Si NPs. As shown in Figs. 3A and Si 2p, Si 2s, C 1s, N 1s and O 1s with their corresponding peaks at 101.6, 153.1, 284.8, 399.4 and 532.0 eV were presented by the full range of XPS analysis [34]. Fig. 3 B revealed the C 1s spectrum and showed different kinds of carbon atoms: C–O, C–N, CC/C–C and C–Si, with their corresponding peaks at 285.8 eV, 285.1 eV, 284.5 eV and 283.9 eV, respectively [34]. Peaks at 398.4, 399.0, and 399.7 eV were shown by high resolution N 1s (Fig. 3C) and they were assigned to Si–N–Si, C–N–C and Si–N–O respectively [23]. Such results confirmed that there were abundant amino groups on the surface of the synthesized Si NPs, coinciding to the corresponding FT-IR spectrum. XPS spectrum of O 1s showed three distinct oxygen atoms, two Si–O (531.2 eV, 531.9 eV) and C–OH/C–O–C (532.6 eV) (Fig. 3D) [33,35]. The high resolution Si 2p spectrum in Fig. 3E presented Si–C, Si–N and Si–O bonds at 101.4, 102.1 and 102.7 eV respectively [36]. Furthermore, the FL spectra and UV–vis absorption spectrum were analyzed to confirm the optical properties of the prepared Si NPs (Fig. 3F). Two absorption bands centered at 239 and 344 nm were received from the UV–vis absorption spectrum. The peaks at 344 nm could be assigned to the trapping surface state, which give rise to the strong emission [23]. The peak at 239 nm was attributed to n-σ* transition of C–N or C–O bond [23]. The prepared Si NPs exhibited green FL under UV irradiation. Under the distinct excitation wavelengths (360–440 nm), the FL emission spectra of the Si NPs were demonstrated in Fig. S5. The maximum emission wavelength was at 515 nm under the excitation wavelength at 400 nm, and the prepared Si NPs revealed the absolute quantum yield of 2.28%.

Stability of the synthesized Si NPs was firstly studied after exposing them to the light illumination during 60 min. It is illustrated in Fig. 4 A that the FL intensity stayed the same, confirming the storage stability. Then, Si NPs were incubated in ionic medium made of NaCl within different concentrations from 0 to 100 mM. Strong salt tolerability was illustrated in Fig. 4B, where the FL intensity remained the same for all concentrations. The stability of the prepared Si NPs was also investigated by studying their FL intensity after their incubation into different temperatures from 25 °C to 85 °C. As illustrated in Fig. 4C, the FL intensity stayed constant. Finally, the effect of pH change to the FL intensity was investigated (Fig. 4D). It is clearly seen that Si NPs had stable and strong FL in the pH range from 4.0 to 10. Only a slight minor dependence of the FL intensity on pH was observed, which should be attributed to the variation in surface charge of Si NPs owing to protonation-deprotonation [37,38]. Above results suggested the excellent stability of the prepared Si NPs, allowing their suitability in different practical researches.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of Si NPs towards Co2+ measurement
ce:section.ce:para: Addition of different concentrations of cobalt ion to the synthesized Si NPs solution revealed the quenching of the FL intensity. The sensitivity of Co2+ measurement was improved by optimizing the solution pH and the reaction time. It is illustrated in Fig. S6A that the pH effect was investigated in the range from 4.0 to 10. As the pH value increased from 4.0 to 7.4, the value of ΔF (F 0–F) increased; and then that slightly decreased as continuing to increase pH (F 0 and F were the FL intensities of the Si NPs at 515 nm before and after addition of Co2+ respectively). The FL intensities were also recorded in the range of time from 0 to 10 min, and three different Co2+ concentrations (10, 25 and 40 μM) were studied and all revealed almost the unchangeable results (Fig. S6B). After optimizing the above conditions, the selected pH and reaction time were 7.4 and 4 min respectively.

Under these optimized parameters, the analysis of cobalt with different concentrations from 0 to 200 μM was studied using the prepared Si NPs. As illustrated in Fig. 5 A, the decreasing of FL intensity was remarked when the concentration of cobalt was increasing. Between the quenching efficiencies (F 0–F)/F 0 and Co2+ concentration, a good linear relationship was noticed in the range of 0.2–60 μM, and the significant linear correlation coefficient (R 2 = 0.9982) was revealed (Fig. 5B). The limit of detection was 0.14 μM, where calculations were conducted based on s/k = 3 (k is the slope of the calibration curve and s represents the standard deviation of the blank solution). According to Table S1, the calculated limit of detection was lower than some reported literatures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of Si NPs towards Co2+ measurement
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity of Si NPs towards Co2+ measurement was conducted by comparing its FL intensity with those of interfering agents. Different metal ions (Al3+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Ag+, Na+, K+ and Li+), some anions (i.e., I−, Br−, Cl−, NO3 −, CO3 2− and SO4 2−) and some biomolecules (alanine, glucose, glutathione, methionine, catechol, hydroquinone, resorcinol and vitamin B12) were selected as potential interferences. It is clearly shown in Fig. 6 that only the addition of Co2+ revealed a distinct FL quenching. Later on, cobalt ion and interfering agents were mixed into the same sensing system, and the FL effect of Si NPs towards Co2+ showed the minimized interference. Such results revealed a significant selectivity of the suggested approach and produced an excellent qualification for Co2+ measurement in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of cobalt in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed approach was used to measure the amount of Co2+ in industrial waste water, Yellow River water and drinking water samples. The average recoveries of Co2+ reached 97.4–101.0% for industrial waste water sample, 100.4–104.0% for Yellow River water sample and 98.8–101.4% for drinking water sample (Table 1 ). It is clearly seen that the method manifested great and potential applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity assay and cell imaging of exogenous cobalt
ce:section.ce:para: MTT assay was conducted for investigating the cytotoxicity of the Si NPs toward HepG2 cells. It was clearly demonstrated in Fig. S7 that when the incubation concentration of the Si NPs increased to 200 μg mL−1, the cell viability was around 89% and the slight damage just began at the concentration of 400 μg mL−1, where the cell viability was estimated to be 72%. This confirmed the synthesized Si NPs were biocompatible and low toxic, and had the great potential for cell imaging. The availability of the synthesized Si NPs probe for sensing exogenous cobalt in living HepG2 cells was demonstrated by conducting the cell imaging experiments. As illustrated in Fig. 7 , after the incubation of HepG2 cells with Si NPs (20 μg mL−1) for 2 h at 37 °C, the significant green emission was revealed. The fluorescence was quickly quenched after addition of cobalt ions (100 μM). The mentioned results confirmed that Si NPs could be a platform for exogenous Co2+ analysis.

In recent years, the FL quenching method was widely employed to measure heavy metal ions. Generally, static quenching effect (SQE), dynamic quenching effect (DQE), Förster resonant energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect (IFE) are the main known effects that can cause the FL quenching of fluorescent materials [39,40]. The quenching is said to be IFE or FRET when there is an overlapping between the UV–vis absorption spectra of the quencher and the emission or excitation spectra of the fluorophore [23]. DQE is a type of quenching mechanism rising from the collision between the fluorophore and the quencher; where the FL pathway is lost and go back to the ground state, as result of such association. A fluorophore is said to be statically quenched (SQE), when it forms a stable complex with another molecule [41].

The quenching of Si NPs by transition metal ions is a complex procedure, therefore, different processes were studied to reveal how the FL intensity emitted by our synthesized Si NPs could be quenched by Co2+ [42]. The study on FL spectra and UV–vis absorption spectrum in Fig. 8 A revealed that there was no overlapping between the emission or excitation spectrum of Si NPs and the absorption spectrum of Co2+, so the energy transferred from Si NPs to Co2+ was ruled out. This testifies that FRET and IFE could not be suspected as quenching mechanisms of fluorescence emitted by our synthesized Si NPs.

Furthermore, the Stern-Volmer plot was used to interpret the quenching results. Temperature dependence in quenching mechanism is involved, where both static and dynamic quenching fit to the Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. (1)) [43]:
(1)
F
0
/ F = 1 +
K
q
τ
0
[ C ] = 1 +
K
sv
[ C ]
where [C] is the concentration of the quencher (Co2+); K SV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, which is the slope of the line; F and F 0 are the steady-state fluorescence intensities in the presence and absence of the quencher, respectively; K q stands for the FL quenching rate constant, reflecting effects of inter-collision and inter-diffusion in this system. K q is calculated as:
(2)
K
q
=
K
sv
/
τ
0
where τ 0 stands for the average lifetime of the fluorescence molecules (Si NPs in our work) without the quencher. The average fluorescence lifetime of our synthesized Si NPs is 3.34 ns (see Table S2) [44]. According to Eq. (2), the calculated K q values were 6.02 × 1012, 5.57 × 1012 and 4.97 × 1012 L mol−1 s−1, corresponding to their temperature at 293, 303 and 313 K respectively (see Table 2 ).

Previous reports have indicated that the quenching would belong to a combined (dynamic and static) mechanism, when the plot of F 0/F versus [C] (Stern-Volumer plot) in a given range of concentration shows an ascending curvature [45,46]. The quenching mechanism will be single dynamic or static, when the plot of F 0/F versus [C] would be linear [45]. In our work, as shown in Fig. S8, a good linearity is observed between F 0/F and Co2+ concentration in the range 0.2–60 μM. From Table 2, increasing the temperature decreased the slope (K sv) and quenching rate constant (K q), and all the calculated K q values were greater than the maximum collision quenching constant K q of various quenchers (2.0 × 1010 L mol−1 s−1) [44], which indicated that the quenching fitted the behavior of static quenching [43,46,47].

The time-resolved FL experiment was also investigated to confirm the mechanism. It is clearly seen from Fig. 8B and Table S2 that the lifetime of the synthesized Si NPs has not varied upon the increase of Co2+ concentration, which also exclude the dynamic quenching. The negligible change of fluorescence lifetimes for Si NPs with Co2+ suggests that the quenching process was attributed to static quenching [48,49].

Furthermore, the mechanism was examined by studying the change of zeta-potential. Upon the addition of Co2+, the zeta-potential of Si NPs increased from −2.92 mV to 1.04 mV (Fig. S9), resulting from the interaction between Si NPs and Co2+ [33]. That interaction was also investigated by the change of full range XPS spectra of Si NPs before and after the addition of Co2+ [33]. As shown in Fig. S10, after adding Co2+, a new peak was created at 778.2 eV. Such spectral change could be related to the interaction between Si NPs and Co2+ [33].

To conclude, a new fluorescent approach based on environment-friendly Si NPs was designed for Co2+ determination. The present approach was cheap, rapid and simple compared to other reported approaches. The synthesized Si NPs revealed pH-stability, salt tolerance and excellent biocompatibility, and they were used to imaging exogenous cobalt ion in HepG2 cells. Furthermore, they were also good analytical platform for Co2+ in Yellow River water, drinking water and industrial wastewater samples.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21874060, 21475053).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

Some additional Figs (S1 to S10) and Tables (S1 to S2) were provided in supporting information.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2019.120636.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.
